comments.text: I have just uploaded my paper on $g_k(N)$. In it, the following bounds are proven: \begin{align*} g_3(N) &= 1 \text{ for all } N \ge 3, \\ g_4(N) &= 3 \text{ for all } N \ge 2, \\ g_5(N) &< 1.2 \cdot 10^8 \text{ for all } N \ge 1, \\ g_k(N) &< 4N^{1 - 2^{2-k}} \text{ for all } k \ge 3 \text{ and all large enough } N. \end{align*}Furthermore, these results are all verified in Lean. The paper is my own work (mostly relying on the ideas by Choi-Erdős-Szemerédi), but specifically the $g_4$ result was obtained with the help of ChatGPT. Further noteworthy is that the bounds on $g_3$ and $g_5$ seemingly contradict the earlier claims of $g_3 = 2$ and $g_5(N) \asymp \log N$. To reconcile this apparent contradiction, let us (as in my previous comment) define $h_k(N)$ as the smallest integer such that for all subsets $A \subseteq \{1, 2, \ldots, 2N\}$ with $|A| \ge N + h_k(N)$ there exist distinct positive integers $b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_k$ such that all $\binom{k}{2}$ pairwise sums are in $A$. As it turns out, $g_k$ and $h_k$ are not the same (in fact, they seem to differ quite often), although we do in general have $$g_k(n) \le h_k(n) \le g_{k+1}(n)$$for all $k$ and $n$. And even though the stated Choi-Erdős-Szemerédi results do not necessarily all hold for $g_k$, they are all recovered for $h_k$. Furthermore, with the help of Aristotle I managed to make their $h_4 \ll 1$ result explicit, while the above general bound for $g_k$ also holds for $h_k$. For completeness' sake, these are the best known bounds for $h_k$ currently: \begin{align*} h_3(N) &= 2 \text{ for all } N \ge 4, \\ h_4(N) &\le 2270 \text{ for all } N \ge 1, \\ h_5(N) &\asymp \log N, \\ h_6(N) &\asymp \sqrt{N} (\text{which is also the correct order of growth for } g_6(N)), \\ h_k(N) &< 4N^{1 - 2^{2-k}} \text{ for all } k \ge 3 \text{ and all large enough } N. \end{align*}Finally, this problem is also mentioned in [Er72, p. 83], which can be added as a reference.
Congrats! First time seeing someone using a nonreasoning model (GPT-5.3 Instant) successfully in research, but I suppose whatever works, works.

It is clear that the $b_i$ need to be distinct, and maybe one can add this requirement to the problem statement for completeness' sake. What is less clear however, is whether the $b_i$ need to be positive. In all theorem statements in [CES75], they refer to the elements in $A \subseteq \{1, 2, \ldots, 2N\}$ as positive integers, while the $b_i$ are just referred to as integers. This terminology is also used in [Er92c, p.41], suggesting that the $b_i$ are not required to be positive (although one can note that at most one can be non-positive, otherwise we have a negative sum). With this in mind, define $h_k(N)$ as the smallest integer such that for all subsets $A \subseteq \{1, 2, \ldots, 2N\}$ with $|A| \ge N + h_k(N)$ there exist distinct positive integers $b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_k$ such that all $\binom{k}{2}$ pairwise sums are in $A$. With $g_k(N)$ defined as in the problem statement (i.e. without the positivity requirement), in general we then have $h_{k-1}(N) \le g_k(N) \le h_k(N)$. Annoyingly, $g_k(N)$ and $h_k(N)$ are not the same function, and the lower bounds that Choi, Erdős and Szemerédi claim for $k = 3$ and $k = 5$ do not actually work for $g_k(N)$. In particular, the example (mentioned at the end of the remarks) of taking $A \subset \{1, 2, \ldots, 2N\}$ to be the union $$\{1, 3, \ldots, 2N-1\} \cup \{2, 4, \ldots, 2^{\lfloor \log_2 2N \rfloor} \}$$for a set for which supposedly no $b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_5$ exist is incorrect as stated; with $b_1 = -1$, $b_2 = 2$, $b_3 = 3$, $b_4 = 5$, $b_5 = 6$, one can check that all pairwise sums do belong to $A$ whenever $N \ge 6$. The example does work to show the lower bound $h_5(N) \gg \log N$, but at the moment I have no non-trivial lower bound for $g_5(N)$. As far as I know, it could be the case that we actually have $g_5(N) \ll 1$ (Edit 06-04-2026: which I might be able to show now!). Turning to $k = 3$, the truth is that $g_k(N)$ is equal to $1$ for all $N \ge 3$. Which is in contrast to the equality $h_3(N) = 2$ that Choi, Erdős and Szemerédi actually (unwittingly?) prove for all $N \ge 4$. To see why $g_3(N) \le 1$ (and therefore $g_3(N) = 1$) holds for all $N \ge 3$, let $A \subseteq \{1, 2, \ldots, 2N\}$ be any set with $|A| \ge N + 1$, and assume $N \ge 3$. Then $A$ contains $\{m, m+1\}$ for some odd $m \in \{1, 3, \ldots, 2N-1\}$ by the pigeonhole principle. If $A \setminus \{m \}$ only contains even integers, then $\{2, 4, 6\} \subseteq \{2, 4, \ldots, 2N\} \subset A$ and we can choose $b_1 = 0$, $b_2 = 2$, $b_3 = 4$. On the other hand, if $A$ contains an odd integer $2j+1 \neq m$, then we can choose $b_1 = j$, $b_2 = j+1$, $b_3 = m-j$, which are quickly checked to be distinct by the assumption $2j+1 \neq m$ and the fact that $m$ is odd. By the way, for $N = 2$ one can note that the example $A = \{2, 3, 4\}$ shows $g_3(2) = 2$. I'll hopefully have more to say in the not too distant future, but I first wanted to clear up the above subtlety.

EDIT 04-05-2026: the bound below is subsumed by the result in this paper I just uploaded. I got interested in trying to find an explicit upper bound for $g_4(N)$. It should still be considered a work in progress, but what I have written so far can be found here, and gives an upper bound of $g_4(N) \le 2032$. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
